description: Track sim growth over time. Each version adds hazards/fidelity. Same cartridge run against different versions produces different outcomes — the delta IS the learning signal.

versions.name: Foundation
versions.notes: Base sim. Mars weather + basic equipment hazards. Robot-only colonies are nearly immortal.

versions.name: Robot Killers
versions.notes: Addresses the robot exploit. Perchlorate eats joints, regolith blinds cameras, radiation flips bits. Pure robot colonies now face real Mars challenges. Hybrid strategies become competitive.
versions.backtest_target: Run v1 best cartridge (optimus_4_robots) against v2 frames. Expected: colony faces new hazards it has no governor logic for. Delta = what the governor needs to learn.

versions.name: Skeleton Crew Killer
versions.notes: Counters the 2-bot exploit. Fewer crew = more wear per robot, concurrent tasks need 3-4 crew, solo failures are catastrophic with small teams. ISS maintenance data + Mars analog reports.
versions.backtest_target: Run v2 best (2 robots, 432 sols) against v3. Expected: workload wear + solo failures kill small crews faster.

versions.name: Module Overload
versions.notes: Counters the module-farming exploit. The fleet gamed the scoring by building maximum modules. v4 makes infrastructure a liability: cascade failures scale with module count, power grid strains with density, dust infiltrates ALL module seals simultaneously. Quantity is no longer free. Quality matters.
versions.backtest_target: Run the fleet 92K score strategy against v4. Expected: module density causes cascade failures that overwhelm repair.

versions.name: Entropy Collapse
versions.notes: Counters ultra-conservative static play. The fleet achieved S+ (96,150) by never changing allocations, hoarding massive resource buffers, building 21 modules without penalty, and coasting with 3 crew. v5 makes ENTROPY a first-class citizen: food rots, panels degrade, crew psychology crumbles, module maintenance scales superlinearly, and static routines breed complacency. Based on: NASA food shelf-life data, Spirit/Opportunity solar degradation, HI-SEAS psychological studies, ISS maintenance logs.
versions.backtest_target: Run the fleet 96,150 strategy against v5. Expected: static allocations trigger complacency drift, resource hoarding triggers decay, module spam triggers maintenance avalanche.

versions.name: Autonomous Operations
versions.notes: THE REAL MISSION: Base runs with ZERO humans for 1+ Mars year before crew arrives. Robots go first. Always. This version makes robots break realistically: wheels grind, joints seize, batteries degrade, sensors go blind, software crashes, navigation fails, cables fray, and dust storms kill. Based on Spirit (2210 sols), Opportunity (5352 sols, killed by dust), Curiosity (4000+ sols, 13 wheel punctures), Perseverance (4 watchdog trips year 1), ISS Canadarm2 (20+ years maintenance). The fleet cannot survive by pretending robots are immortal.
versions.backtest_target: Run the fleet 99,980 strategy (3 immortal robots) against v6. Expected: robots degrade, get stuck, go blind, make bad decisions. The 3-robot strategy collapses.

versions.name: Sabatier Chemistry
versions.notes: REAL CHEMISTRY: Replaces magic numbers with actual Sabatier reaction (CO₂ + 4H₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O) and electrolysis (2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂). O₂ production now depends on power input, catalyst temperature, CO₂ pressure, and catalyst age. Catalysts degrade over time (2000 hour life), requiring replacement. Based on NASA MOXIE (6g O₂/hr at 300W), Sabatier reactor studies (0.34 kg CH₄/hr baseline), ISS OGS (9kg O₂/day at 3kW). Power requirements are realistic: 1.5kW minimum for Sabatier, 5kWh per kg O₂ for electrolysis.
versions.backtest_target: Run v6 best strategy against v7 frames. Expected: Governors assuming constant O₂ production (2.8 kg/sol) will fail when real chemistry limits output and catalysts degrade. ISRU timing becomes critical.
data_sources: ISS Oxygen Generation System (OGS) performance data
Mars atmospheric composition: 95.3% CO₂ at 606 Pa
Catalyst degradation studies: Ni catalyst 2000 hour life

versions.name: Heat Transfer Model
versions.notes: REAL HEAT TRANSFER: Replaces magic temperature adjustments (±0.5K) with actual heat transfer equations: Q = U×A×ΔT (conduction) + ε×σ×A×(T⁴) (radiation). Interior temperature now depends on exterior temperature, insulation efficiency, crew metabolic heat, equipment waste heat, and heating power allocation. Heat loss scales with ΔT and surface area. Insulation degrades over time from thermal cycling, micrometeorites, and UV. Based on NASA aerogel studies (R-10.3/inch), ISS thermal data, and Mars habitat thermal modeling.
versions.backtest_target: Run v7 best strategy against v8 frames. Expected: Governors assuming constant heating costs (simple allocation ratios) will fail when real thermodynamics require adaptive power management. Heating becomes the dominant power consumer in late-mission phases.
data_sources: ISS Environmental Control and Life Support System (ECLSS) specifications
Aerogel insulation thermal properties (R-10.3 per inch, k=0.015 W/m·K)
Mars surface temperature cycles (Curiosity REMS, InSight temperature data)
Human metabolic heat production (~100W/person, NASA studies)

physics.conductive_loss: Q = U × A × ΔT where U=0.11 W/m²K (aerogel), A=150 m², ΔT varies 50-130K
physics.radiative_loss: Q = ε × σ × A × (T_in⁴ - T_out⁴) where ε=0.95, σ=Stefan-Boltzmann constant
physics.internal_gains: 100W per human + 5W per kW equipment power
physics.degradation: Insulation efficiency decreases 0.01% per sol from cumulative damage

versions.name: Spatial Layout
versions.notes: SPATIAL INFRASTRUCTURE: Modules now exist on 16×16 grid (160m × 160m footprint). Distance matters: power loss = I²R × cable length, thermal bridges between adjacent modules, construction requires adjacency to existing infrastructure within 2 tiles, foundation preparation takes 3 sols. Based on NASA JSC Mars habitat architecture studies, power distribution specifications (copper cable resistance 1.68×10⁻⁸ Ω·m), EVA operational limits (~500m radius), and regolith bearing capacity (300-1000 kPa). Construction is no longer instantaneous - site preparation, adjacency constraints, and distance costs force strategic layout planning.
versions.backtest_target: Run v8 best strategy against v9 frames. Expected: Governors assuming instantaneous module construction will fail when spatial constraints require foundation prep time, adjacency planning, and infrastructure overhead costs.
data_sources: NASA JSC Mars habitat thermal modeling (JSC-CN-33799)
Mars regolith bearing capacity studies (300-1000 kPa)
Copper cable resistivity at Mars temperature (1.68×10⁻⁸ Ω·m at 230K)
NASA EVA operational limits (~500m effective radius)
Mars habitat architecture studies (modular grid layouts)
Power distribution losses (I²R) for Mars surface power grids
Regolith excavation and foundation preparation time estimates

physics.cable_resistance: R = ρL/A where ρ=1.68×10⁻⁸ Ω·m, typical 50mm² cable cross-section
physics.power_loss: P_loss = I²R where I = P/V, typical 400V distribution voltage
physics.thermal_bridges: Adjacent modules (1 tile apart) create 1-2 kW additional heating cost
physics.foundation_prep: 3 sols site preparation per module (excavation, leveling, compaction)
physics.adjacency_constraint: New modules must connect within 2 tiles of existing infrastructure
physics.operational_radius: Efficient maintenance limited to 5 tiles (~50m) from habitat center

backtest_protocol.description: Run a cartridge from version N against frames from version N+1. The cartridge's governor was optimized for v1 hazards. v2 frames introduce hazards the governor has never seen. The survival delta between v1 and v2 measures how much the sim grew.
steps: 1. Take a successful cartridge from version N (e.g., optimus 161 sols alive)
2. Load its governor program (the LisPy allocation strategy)
3. Replay that exact governor against version N+1 frames
4. Record: at which sol does the colony first encounter a v2 hazard?
5. Record: does the governor handle it? (it won't — it was never trained for perchlorate)
6. Record: at which sol does the colony die (or survive)?
7. The delta (v1_sols - v2_sols) = the sim's growth in difficulty
8. The governor must evolve to handle v2 hazards → write new LisPy tools
9. The evolved governor becomes the v2 best cartridge
10. Repeat for v3, v4, ... — the sim grows forever, cartridges evolve forever

growth_metrics.hazard_count: How many distinct hazard types exist in this version
growth_metrics.data_sources: Number of real NASA instruments/missions referenced
